topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The thick, cream-colored envelope sat on the edge of my desk, a stark intrusion in the otherwise meticulously organized chaos of my workspace. I picked it up with two fingers, the paper feeling brittle beneath my touch. Inside, the script was formal, painstakingly worded, a full-blown mea culpa spanning several pages. It detailed regret, acknowledged the damage, and offered a sincere, if belated, apology for an event that had happened nearly a decade ago. I reread the opening sentence twice. A slow, almost imperceptible tightening began in my jaw. I let the letter drop onto the blotter with a soft thud, the sound echoing disproportionately loud in the quiet office. I stood up, pacing the length of the room, my footsteps sharp against the hardwood floor. I needed a distraction, anything to break this lingering tension.

[story 2]

When the post arrived, I barely glanced at the address. It was from him. After all this time, a formal acknowledgment of his mistake. I tore it open with unnecessary force, the crisp edges protesting the sudden violence. My eyes scanned the text, cataloging the practiced sincerity. A low, involuntary hum escaped my throat. I crumpled the paper into a tight, furious ball, the creases digging into my palm. I tossed it toward the recycling bin, missing by a fraction, and caught it again, smoothing the wrinkles out with impatient, jerky movements. I stared at the bin, my breathing shallow and quickening. Why now? The sheer timing of it grated on my nerves like sandpaper. I needed to get out, needed noise, needed distance from this suffocating reminder.

[story 3]

I found the letter tucked inside a forgotten book, the paper yellowed at the edges. It was a full confession, an extensive account of remorse for something that had caused considerable trouble years ago. I read it once, then twice more, my gaze skimming the carefully constructed sentences as if trying to find the flaw in the logic. A distinct stiffness settled in my shoulders, pulling my neck taut. I set the letter down abruptly, pushing it away as if it were contaminated. I walked over to the window, staring out at the indifferent city traffic below. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the muscles in my forearms flexing under the strain. The silence in the room felt heavy, charged with an unwelcome obligation.
